articles.title: 10 Practical Ways to Cope With Cravings in Early Recovery
articles.summary: Step-by-step strategies you can use in the moment when a craving hits, including urge surfing and grounding techniques.
articles.body: Cravings are a normal part of early recovery, but they don’t have to control your actions. In this guide, we’ll walk through practical tools you can use the moment a craving appears...

articles.title: Coping With Nighttime Cravings: A 7-Step Checklist
articles.summary: A quick checklist you can follow when cravings feel strongest at night.
articles.body: Many people notice that cravings get stronger in the evening, when they’re tired or alone. This article offers a simple 7-step checklist you can keep by your bed or on your phone...

articles.title: Urge Surfing: Riding Out Cravings Without Giving In
articles.summary: Learn how to observe cravings like waves instead of fighting or acting on them.
articles.body: Urge surfing is a mindfulness-based technique that helps you experience cravings without reacting to them. Instead of trying to push cravings away, you learn to notice the sensations rise, peak, and fall...

articles.title: Handling Cravings at Work Without Anyone Noticing
articles.summary: Discreet ways to manage cravings during the workday while staying focused and professional.
articles.body: Work can be a high-pressure environment, and for many people in recovery, it’s also full of triggers. This article focuses on subtle tools you can use at your desk, in meetings, or on breaks...

articles.title: From Trigger to Choice: A Short Guide to Craving Logs
articles.summary: How to track your cravings so you can predict and prevent them over time.
articles.body: Keeping a craving log is a simple but powerful way to understand your patterns. By writing down when, where, and how cravings show up, you gain data you can act on...

articles.title: Staying Sober Through Social Events
articles.summary: Practical boundaries and exit plans for parties, weddings, and work events.
articles.body: Social events can be full of triggers—alcohol, old using friends, and social pressure. Planning ahead dramatically reduces your risk of relapse...

articles.title: Understanding Post-Acute Withdrawal Syndrome (PAWS)
articles.summary: Why mood swings, sleep problems, and low energy can last for months—and what to do about it.
articles.body: Post-acute withdrawal syndrome (PAWS) can last weeks or months after you stop using. Recognizing the symptoms helps you plan care and avoid relapse...

articles.title: A Family Guide to Supporting a Loved One in Early Recovery
articles.summary: Concrete do’s and don’ts for partners, parents, and close friends.
articles.body: Families often want to help but aren’t sure how. This guide offers specific language, boundaries, and examples that support recovery without enabling...

articles.title: Mindful Breathing for Cravings: A 5-Minute Practice
articles.summary: A short, guided breathing exercise you can use anywhere when cravings spike.
articles.body: Mindful breathing can interrupt the automatic cycle between craving and use. This article includes a simple 4-2-6 breathing pattern and tips for practice...

articles.title: Creating a Weekly Recovery Plan That Actually Works
articles.summary: How to balance meetings, self-care, and responsibilities without burning out.
articles.body: A realistic weekly plan can turn vague intentions into concrete actions. We’ll walk through how to schedule meetings, recovery tasks, and rest...

articles.title: Grounding Techniques for Anxiety and Cravings
articles.summary: Use your five senses to come back to the present when you feel overwhelmed.
articles.body: Grounding exercises help your nervous system settle when anxiety and cravings spike. This article outlines several 5-senses techniques...

articles.title: Is Online Recovery Support Right for You?
articles.summary: Pros and cons of online meetings, forums, and apps for sobriety.
articles.body: Online support can make recovery more accessible, but it’s not the same as in-person connection. This article compares formats and helps you decide...

articles.title: Managing Cravings During Holidays and Special Occasions
articles.summary: Plan ahead for high-risk days like holidays, birthdays, and anniversaries.
articles.body: Holidays can mix joy, grief, and pressure—conditions where cravings thrive. Learn how to create a relapse prevention plan for those dates...

articles.title: What to Do After a Slip: Getting Back on Track Without Shame
articles.summary: A compassionate, step-by-step guide for responding to a relapse or slip.
articles.body: A slip does not erase your progress. This article helps you respond to relapse with honesty and self-compassion while tightening your recovery plan...

events.title: Saturday Relapse Prevention Skills Workshop (Online)
events.description: A live, interactive workshop focused on building relapse prevention plans for the next 30 days. Includes craving management, risk mapping, and support planning.

events.title: Relapse Prevention Bootcamp: Building Your Safety Plan
events.description: A half-day bootcamp covering triggers, warning signs, and emergency coping strategies. Includes downloadable worksheets.

events.title: Weekend Relapse Prevention Group Intensive
events.description: A small-group intensive focusing on relapse warning signs and accountability. Includes follow-up check-in call.

events.title: Mindfulness for Craving Management
events.description: An online workshop teaching mindfulness practices specifically for managing cravings and anxiety.

events.title: Family Support Evening: Understanding Addiction
events.description: A live Q&A for families wanting to better understand addiction and how to support recovery.

events.title: Building Coping Skills for High-Risk Days
events.description: Learn specific coping skills to handle birthdays, holidays, and anniversaries without using.

events.title: Relapse Prevention Planning for 6–12 Months Sober
events.description: Workshop tailored for people with 6–12 months sobriety, focusing on complacency and burnout.

events.title: Intro to Mindful Breathing for Recovery
events.description: A beginner-friendly introduction to mindful breathing techniques that support sobriety.

events.title: Resilience for Families: Boundaries and Burnout
events.description: Helps family members and partners set healthy limits and care for themselves.

events.title: Coping Skills Lab: Managing Anxiety Without Substances
events.description: Interactive skills lab focused on non-substance ways to manage anxiety, such as grounding and opposite action.

events.title: Relapse Prevention Basics for Early Recovery
events.description: An introductory workshop on recognizing relapse warning signs within the first 90 days.

events.title: Mindful Mornings: Daily Practice Group
events.description: A recurring morning mindfulness session to start your day grounded in recovery.

events.title: Creative Recovery: Art as a Coping Skill
events.description: Hands-on art activities to process emotions and cravings in a safe, supportive space.

events.title: Couples in Recovery: Communication Workshop
events.description: Tools for couples navigating recovery together, including conflict de-escalation and boundary setting.

facilitators.name: Sarah Nguyen, LCSW
facilitators.bio: Sarah is a licensed clinical social worker with 9 years of experience facilitating women’s addiction recovery groups, with a focus on trauma-informed care and early sobriety.

facilitators.name: Michael Thompson, CADC
facilitators.bio: Michael has been in long-term recovery since 2010 and leads mixed-gender groups focused on relapse prevention and coping skills.

facilitators.name: Ana Rodriguez, Peer Recovery Coach
facilitators.bio: Ana facilitates bilingual (English/Spanish) online support groups for women and LGBTQ+ people in early recovery.

facilitators.name: David Kim, PhD
facilitators.bio: David is a psychologist specializing in mindfulness-based relapse prevention and runs weekly meditation groups.

facilitators.name: Rachel Cohen, LMFT
facilitators.bio: Rachel works with families and partners impacted by addiction and co-facilitates a family support group.

hotlines.name: National Substance Use Crisis Line
hotlines.description: Confidential support, information, and referrals for any substance use concern across the United States.

hotlines.name: Recovery Helpline USA
hotlines.hours_description: 24 hours a day, 7 days a week
hotlines.description: Call for emotional support, crisis de-escalation, and connection to local treatment resources.

hotlines.name: All-Substance Text & Call Line
hotlines.description: Text or call for immediate help with cravings, urges to use, or relapse situations involving any substance.

hotlines.name: National Recovery Warmline
hotlines.description: Peer-run support line staffed by people in recovery. Provides non-judgmental listening and resources.

hotlines.name: Alcohol Recovery Hotline
hotlines.description: Specialized support for alcohol-related concerns, including withdrawal questions and treatment options.

hotlines.name: Opioid Overdose & Naloxone Line
hotlines.description: Real-time support for suspected opioid overdoses, naloxone use, and post-overdose safety planning.

hotlines.name: Statewide Addiction Support Line - Colorado
hotlines.description: Information and referral to treatment and recovery supports within Colorado.

hotlines.name: Local Recovery Center Helpline - Denver
hotlines.description: Run by a local recovery community center in Denver; offers walk-in support options.

hotlines.name: Mental Health & Suicide Crisis Line
hotlines.description: Immediate help for suicidal thoughts, severe depression, and other mental health crises.

hotlines.name: Family & Friends Support Line
hotlines.description: Support and coaching for loved ones of people struggling with addiction.

hotlines.name: Spanish-Language Recovery Hotline
hotlines.description: Spanish-speaking counselors offering crisis support and recovery information for all substance use.

hotlines.name: Youth Substance Use Helpline
hotlines.description: Confidential help for teens and young adults dealing with substance use and peer pressure.

self_assessment_quizzes.title: Addiction Recovery Self-Assessment
self_assessment_quizzes.description: A brief questionnaire about your substance use history, cravings, and support system that generates personalized group and meeting recommendations.

self_assessment_quizzes.title: Relapse Risk Check-In
self_assessment_quizzes.description: Assess your current relapse risk based on stress, triggers, and coping strategies, and get suggestions for workshops and tools.

self_assessment_quizzes.title: Old Recovery Readiness Survey
self_assessment_quizzes.description: A legacy survey about readiness for change. Kept for archival purposes.

stories.title: Thirty Days Sober: How I Got Through the First Month
stories.excerpt: In my first 30 days, cravings hit hardest at night. Here’s what actually helped me stay sober one day at a time.
stories.body:
When I walked into my first meeting, I was shaking. I hadn’t gone a single night without drinking in years. The first week felt impossible...

I started making a list of three things I would do before I even considered drinking: text my sponsor, make tea, and take a shower...

stories.title: White-Knuckling No More: Finding Support in Week Two
stories.excerpt: At two weeks sober, I thought I had to do it all alone. A women-only group changed that.
stories.body:
By day 14, I was exhausted from pretending I was fine. My hands literally shook on my commute home. I told myself I just had to “be strong,” but strength isn’t white-knuckling...

A coworker slipped me a flyer for a women’s support group. I almost threw it away...

stories.title: From Daily Use to Day 60: Small Habits That Made a Big Difference
stories.excerpt: I didn’t overhaul my life overnight. I changed it with tiny, boring habits I could actually keep.
stories.body:
My last day using was a Tuesday. By Friday, I was already planning my relapse. The only reason I didn’t was because my counselor asked me to try one small habit instead of “fixing everything.”...

I started with drinking water before coffee and texting someone from my group every morning...

stories.title: Early Recovery With Kids at Home
stories.excerpt: Staying sober with toddlers is chaos. These are the boundaries that kept me and my kids safe.
stories.body: I used to drink after my kids went to bed. When I stopped, the evenings felt endless and loud. I realized I needed new routines for all of us...

stories.title: My First 24 Hours Without Opioids
stories.excerpt: I didn’t think I could survive 24 hours without pills. Here’s how a crisis line and one meeting got me through.
stories.body: I had the crisis hotline number saved in my phone for months. I finally dialed it shaking at 3am. The person who answered didn’t judge me once...

stories.title: One Year Later: What I Wish I Knew at 30 Days
stories.excerpt: At a year sober, I see my first month differently. Here’s the advice I’d give that version of me.
stories.body: I used to think slipping meant starting at zero. By a year sober, I realized recovery is more like learning a language...

stories.title: Six Months Sober: Facing My First Holiday Season
stories.excerpt: I made it through the holidays by letting go of perfection and focusing on connection.
stories.body:
I was six months sober when the holiday invitations rolled in. Every one felt like a test I was doomed to fail...

I decided to make a plan with my sponsor for each event...

stories.title: Why I Finally Asked for Help After 15 Years Drinking
stories.excerpt: It wasn’t one big rock bottom. It was a series of tiny quiet moments that told me I couldn’t do this alone.
stories.body: My story doesn’t have a dramatic car crash or arrest. It has empty Tuesday nights and mornings I couldn’t remember...

stories.title: Two Months Without Weed: Clearing the Fog
stories.excerpt: I didn’t think marijuana was a problem until I tried to quit. The mental fog lifting was my biggest surprise.
stories.body: I used to joke that weed wasn’t a “real” addiction. Quitting showed me how tightly it had wrapped around my life...

stories.title: Supporting My Partner Through Detox
stories.excerpt: I thought love meant fixing everything. Recovery taught me that support looks different.
stories.body: When my partner finally agreed to detox, I felt terrified and hopeful at the same time...

stories.title: Relapse at 9 Months: Why I Came Back
stories.excerpt: I almost didn’t return after I relapsed. Shame told me to stay away. Community pulled me back.
stories.body: The night I relapsed, I told myself I’d lost all my time. My first sponsor reminded me that every day sober still counted...

stories.title: Finding My First Sponsor
stories.excerpt: I was sure everyone would say no. Instead, three people said yes in different ways.
stories.body:
The idea of asking someone to be my sponsor felt like proposing marriage. I put it off for weeks...

At my fourth meeting, a woman shared about taking calls at midnight. My heart pounded...

stories.title: Grieving My Old Life While Building a New One
stories.excerpt: Sobriety gave me clarity, but it also meant saying goodbye to people and places I loved.
stories.body: No one warned me how much grief comes with recovery. I missed the bar across the street, even though it almost killed me...

stories.title: My First Online Meeting: Terrified But Grateful
stories.excerpt: I joined with my camera off and my name as just a letter. It still changed everything.
stories.body: I was too anxious to walk into a building, so I tried an online meeting. I logged in early, shaking, and stared at the list of names...

meetings.title: Downtown Denver Wednesday Night Recovery Group
meetings.short_description: In-person open recovery meeting focused on coping with cravings and early sobriety.
meetings.full_description: A weekly in-person meeting in downtown Denver for anyone seeking support with alcohol or other substance use. We focus on practical tools for getting through cravings, building a sober network, and making it through the mid-week. Newcomers are welcome, and you can just listen if you prefer.

meetings.title: Denver Wednesday Evening Narcotics Support (In-Person)
meetings.short_description: A weekly narcotics-focused support group for people in recovery from opioids and other drugs.
meetings.full_description: This in-person group is for anyone recovering from narcotics, including opioids, stimulants, or mixed substances. Meetings include a short reading, open sharing, and time for questions about managing cravings and withdrawal. Ideal for people in their first year but open to all.

meetings.title: Capitol Hill Monday Alcohol Support Group
meetings.short_description: Evening in-person meeting focused on alcohol recovery with step-based discussion.
meetings.full_description: A neighborhood alcohol support group in Denver's Capitol Hill area. The meeting includes a reading related to alcohol recovery, followed by open sharing. Great for people who work during the day and prefer evening support.

meetings.title: Friday Night Family & Friends Support Circle (Online)
meetings.short_description: Online meeting for partners, parents, and friends affected by someone else’s substance use.
meetings.full_description: This weekly online group is for family members and close friends who want a safe space to talk about boundaries, burnout, and hope. Facilitated by a licensed family therapist, meetings include a short topic and open discussion.

meetings.title: Thursday Online All-Substance Recovery Meeting
meetings.short_description: Highly rated online evening meeting open to anyone in recovery from any substance.
meetings.full_description: A structured, topic-focused meeting covering cravings, relapse prevention, and building a daily program. Ideal for people who can’t attend in-person meetings or prefer online support. Includes breakout rooms for optional small-group sharing.

meetings.title: Thursday Late Online Alcohol Support
meetings.short_description: Online alcohol-focused meeting for people who need later evening support.
meetings.full_description: A late evening alcohol support meeting that’s especially helpful for people who find cravings strongest at night. Includes a short reading, a speaker share, and open sharing time.

meetings.title: Thursday Night Beginners' Online Recovery Group
meetings.short_description: Beginner-friendly online meeting explaining basic recovery tools.
meetings.full_description: Designed for people in their first 90 days or anyone returning to recovery after a break. We briefly explain how meetings work, share simple coping skills for cravings, and leave time for questions.

meetings.title: Women’s Online Support Circle (Evening)
meetings.short_description: Women-only online group focused on early recovery, cravings, and self-compassion.
meetings.full_description: A safe, women-only space for those in early recovery or returning after relapse. Meetings include brief grounding exercises, topic discussion, and optional sharing. Cameras can be off, and you’re welcome to just listen.

meetings.title: Women-Only In-Person Recovery Group - Denver
meetings.short_description: Trauma-informed, women-only in-person meeting in central Denver.
meetings.full_description: A weekly in-person group for women seeking support around substance use, trauma, and rebuilding daily routines. Includes a short grounding exercise, guided topic, and open sharing. Childcare is not provided, but infants are welcome.

meetings.title: LGBTQ+ Online Recovery Meeting
meetings.short_description: Online space for LGBTQ+ people seeking support around substance use.
meetings.full_description: A weekly online recovery meeting for LGBTQ+ community members. Topics include identity, shame, family rejection, and building affirming support networks.

meetings.title: Men’s In-Person Step Study - Denver
meetings.short_description: Men-only in-person step study for people working a 12-step program.
meetings.full_description: A weekly men’s group that reads from 12-step literature and discusses one step each week. Focused on honesty, accountability, and building a sober network.

meetings.title: Daily Online Morning Meditation for Recovery
meetings.short_description: Short guided meditation to start the day sober and grounded.
meetings.full_description: A 20-minute daily online meditation group open to anyone in recovery. No experience required. Each session includes a brief check-in, guided practice, and a short closing reading.

meetings.title: Parents in Recovery Hybrid Support Group
meetings.short_description: Hybrid group for parents balancing sobriety, parenting, and stress.
meetings.full_description: A weekly hybrid (in-person + online) meeting for parents in recovery. Each week focuses on a parenting theme, coping with guilt, or setting boundaries at home.

meetings.title: Midday Online Narcotics Support
meetings.short_description: Lunch-hour online group focused on narcotics recovery.
meetings.full_description: An online meeting for people who use their lunch break to connect with others in narcotics recovery. Includes a brief reading and open sharing.

meetings.title: Al-Anon Style Family & Friends Group - Denver
meetings.short_description: In-person evening meeting for people affected by someone else’s drinking or drug use.
meetings.full_description: Modeled after Al-Anon principles, this in-person group is for anyone worried about a loved one’s substance use. Focus on self-care, detachment with love, and practical boundary setting.

self_assessment_questions.question_text: How often are you currently experiencing cravings to use alcohol or other substances? (e.g., Never, Weekly, A few times a week, Daily, Multiple times per day)
When was the last time you used alcohol or another substance? (e.g., Today, Yesterday, 2 days ago, 3 days ago, 4–7 days ago, More than a week ago)
What substance or behavior is your main concern right now?
How many people can you honestly talk to about your recovery? (e.g., No one, 1–2 people, 3–5 people, 6 or more people)
On a scale from 0 to 10, how strong are your cravings on a typical day? (0 = none, 10 = overwhelming)
Which types of support are you interested in right now? (e.g., In-person meetings, Online meetings, Family & friends groups, One-on-one support, Workshops)
What times of day are you usually available for meetings or groups? (Select all that apply: Morning, Afternoon, Early evening, Late evening, Weekends)
How comfortable do you feel sharing in a group setting? (0 = not at all comfortable, 10 = very comfortable)
What is your biggest goal for the next 30 days of your recovery?
Have you ever attended a support group or recovery meeting before? If yes, what did and didn’t work for you?
In the past week, how high has your overall stress level felt? (0 = no stress, 10 = extremely high stress)
Which triggers have shown up for you recently? (e.g., People, Places, Things, Feelings like anger or loneliness, Special occasions)
On a scale from 0 to 10, how confident do you feel using coping skills instead of substances when you are triggered?
When was your most recent slip or relapse, if any? (e.g., In the last 24 hours, 2–3 days ago, 4–7 days ago, More than a week ago, I haven’t had a slip in the last 3 months)
How ready do you feel to make changes to your substance use right now? (0 = not at all ready, 10 = completely ready)
What concerns or fears do you have about starting or returning to recovery support?
